worth_it_explanation: Alternative identifiers such as RampID and Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) are how a publisher carries an addressable signal into the bidstream now that third-party cookies are unreliable. An ID that is configured but not actually passing, or that is passing without the consent it depends on, looks fine on the surface while the addressable demand it should attract never arrives. The check reads the identity setup a publisher already has on Equativ and in its header-bidding stack, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific identifier and a specific gap to close.
intro: Equativ resolves authenticated traffic into RampID (the LiveRamp identifier, formerly IdentityLink) and supports other alternative identifiers such as UID2 and EUID, carrying them into the bidstream so buyers can recognize a consented user without a third-party cookie. This subcategory is about whether those identifiers are configured correctly and reaching demand, not about the broader consent management setup, which the privacy checks cover. These checks confirm that the alternative IDs a publisher relies on are present and passing, and that running more than one ID solution at once is a deliberate choice rather than a source of conflicting or unconsented signals.

audit_checks.title: RampID or other alternative IDs are configured but not passing into the bidstream
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An alternative ID that is set up but absent from bid requests, with no error to flag it, gives a publisher the impression of addressability it does not have. Buyers that bid higher when they can recognize a consented user fall back to a lower contextual bid, so the addressable premium the identifier was meant to capture goes uncollected on inventory the publisher believes is already covered.

educate.base:
RampID is LiveRamp's pseudonymous, people-based identifier, created by hashing and encrypting an authenticated signal such as a logged-in email so a consented user can be recognized across the open internet without a third-party cookie. Equativ resolves authenticated traffic into RampID and can carry other alternative identifiers such as Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) and EUID alongside it. In a header-bidding setup, these identifiers are typically generated by a user ID module and placed into the bid request in the standardized extended identifiers (EIDs) format, so every bidder sees the same signal.

The failure this check looks for is an identifier that is configured but never reaches the bidstream. The module can be installed, the placement registered, and the setup look complete, yet bid requests leave without the ID attached. Common reasons include a storage key that does not match what the module expects, an envelope that never gets written because the authentication step did not fire, a refresh interval that lets the stored value expire, or a consent gate that blocks the ID call before it runs.

The cost is quiet. Demand-side platforms that raise their bid when they can resolve a consented user simply do not see the signal, so they bid as if the impression were anonymous. Fill may look unchanged because the impression still sells, but it sells at a contextual price rather than the addressable one the identifier was meant to earn. Nothing errors, so the gap can sit unnoticed until someone inspects an actual bid request.

A few signs point to an alternative ID that is not passing:
  • A live bid request inspected in the browser carries no EIDs entry for the identifier the publisher believes is configured
  • The expected storage value, e.g., the RampID envelope under its storage key, is empty or missing in the browser after the user authenticates
  • Reporting shows little or no addressable or ID-matched demand on inventory where the identifier is supposed to be active
You may want to confirm that each alternative ID the publisher relies on actually appears in a real bid request rather than only in the configuration. One approach is to inspect a live auction in the browser and check the EIDs array for each identifier the setup claims to carry.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, authenticated identifiers depend on the share of traffic that is logged in, such as newsletter or registered-account visitors, so the ID may pass on the authenticated segment while the bulk of anonymous traffic carries nothing. You may want to read where the identifier appears by section or by logged-in state rather than assuming a single check on the homepage represents the whole site.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the checkout and account flows are where authentication and consent are most likely captured, so an alternative ID can be present on those pages while editorial and category pages pass none. You may want to confirm the identifier reaches the bidstream on the higher-intent commercial pages specifically, where the addressable premium is worth the most.

investigate.intro: Inspect a live auction to see which alternative identifiers actually reach the bid request, and compare that against the identifiers the publisher's setup claims to carry.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Open the site in a browser, sign in or authenticate if the identifier depends on a logged-in state, and open developer tools.
In the 'Network' tab, find an outgoing bid request and read its payload, then locate the user EIDs array and note which identifiers (e.g., RampID, UID2, EUID) appear.
In the 'Application' tab, check storage for the expected identifier values, e.g., the RampID envelope under its storage key, and confirm they are present and not expired.
Compare the identifiers that actually appear against the list the publisher expects to be passing, and record any that are configured but absent.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Review the identity and addressability settings to record which alternative IDs the publisher has enabled and on what inventory.
Confirm the registration details each identifier needs, e.g., the LiveRamp placement ID for RampID, are entered and active.
Record any identifier that is enabled in the console but did not appear in the inspected bid request, which is the gap this check is about.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each alternative ID the publisher relies on, whether it appeared in a real bid request and whether its stored value was present after authentication. Identifiers that are configured and confirmed passing in a live auction pass. An identifier that is enabled in the setup but absent from the bid request, or whose stored value is missing or expired, is a fail recorded with the identifier and the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which alternative IDs appeared in a live bid request and which were configured but absent above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has alternative identifiers configured that are not reaching the bidstream, so a live bid request leaves without the identity signal the setup claims to carry. RampID and identifiers such as Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) let demand-side platforms recognize a consented user and bid the addressable price, and when the signal is absent those buyers bid as if the impression were anonymous, so the premium goes uncollected without any error to flag it. {{voice_recommend}} inspecting a live auction for each identifier the publisher relies on, confirming it appears in the extended identifiers array and that its stored value is present after authentication, then fixing the storage key, refresh interval, or consent gate that is blocking it. After the fix, recheck a real bid request to confirm the identifier passes.

audit_checks.title: Multiple ID solutions run at once without a deliberate priority or deduplication
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Running several identifiers at once is reasonable, but doing it without a stated priority can let two sources populate the same ID type with different values, or pad the bid request with identifiers no buyer uses. The result is an ambiguous signal where buyers cannot tell which value to trust, and added weight in every request, neither of which a deliberate identifier mix would carry.

educate.base:
A publisher often carries more than one alternative identifier at once, e.g., RampID for buyers who transact on LiveRamp and Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) for buyers who transact on UID2. Different demand-side platforms resolve different identity solutions. Carrying several is a sound strategy when each one earns demand. The question this check raises is whether the mix is deliberate or whether identifiers have accumulated without anyone deciding how they should coexist.

Two problems appear when several IDs run without a plan. The first is a collision. When two sources can populate the same identifier type, e.g., two modules both supplying a UID2 value, the bid request needs a rule for which one wins. Prebid's User ID module handles this with an idPriority setting that names the preferred source per identifier, and without it the resolved value can depend on load order rather than on which source the publisher trusts. The second is dead weight. An identifier that no active buyer resolves still gets generated, stored, and attached to every bid request, which adds latency and request size while earning nothing.

The audit goal is to confirm the identifier mix is a set of deliberate choices. Each identifier carried should be one a real buyer uses, each identifier type that more than one source can populate should have a stated priority, and identifiers that no longer earn demand should be retired rather than left running. That keeps the signal in each bid request clean and the request itself lean.

A few signs point to an identifier mix that has not been managed:
  • More than one source can populate the same identifier type with no priority rule, e.g., no idPriority entry, deciding which value wins
  • An identifier appears in bid requests that the publisher cannot tie to any buyer who actually resolves it
  • The list of active identifiers has grown over time with no review of which ones still earn demand
You may want to confirm that every identifier carried is deliberate and that any ID type populated by more than one source has a defined priority. One approach is to list the active identifiers against the buyers that resolve each, set a priority where two sources overlap, and retire any identifier that earns no demand.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that has added identity partners over several years, identifiers from past tests can linger in the stack after the demand that justified them moved on. You may want to map each active identifier to a buyer that resolves it today and retire the ones that no longer earn demand, so the bid request carries only signals that are used.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a customer data partner and a header-bidding module can both supply an identifier built from the same authenticated email, which is exactly the case where two sources populate one ID type. You may want to set a clear priority for which source wins so buyers receive one consistent value rather than two that may disagree.

learn_more.source_title: Module - User ID for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: List the alternative identifiers running at once, check whether any ID type is populated by more than one source, and confirm a priority and a real buyer exist for each.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Open a live bid request in the 'Network' tab and read the user EIDs array to list every identifier source and value currently attached.
Note any identifier type that appears from more than one source, which is where a collision can occur.
Check the User ID module configuration for an idPriority setting and record whether each overlapping ID type has a stated priority or resolves by load order.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Review the enabled identity solutions and record the full list of alternative IDs active across the publisher's inventory.
For each identifier, note the buyers or demand partners that resolve it, and flag any identifier with no buyer tied to it.
Record any identifier that is enabled but no longer earns demand as a candidate to retire.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the active alternative identifiers, the buyers that resolve each, and whether any ID type populated by more than one source has a stated priority. A mix where every identifier ties to a real buyer and every overlapping ID type has a defined priority passes. Two sources populating the same ID type with no priority, or an identifier with no buyer resolving it, is a fail recorded with the identifiers and the conflict or redundancy found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the active identifiers, the buyer resolving each, and the priority for any overlapping ID type above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs several alternative identifiers at once without a stated priority or a review of which ones still earn demand. When more than one source can populate the same identifier type, the resolved value can depend on load order rather than on the source the publisher trusts, and identifiers that no buyer resolves still add weight to every bid request while earning nothing. {{voice_recommend}} listing each active identifier against the buyer that resolves it, setting a priority such as the Prebid idPriority for any ID type two sources can populate, and retiring identifiers that no longer earn demand. After the change, recheck a live bid request to confirm each ID type carries one trusted value and the request holds only identifiers in use.

audit_checks.title: Alternative IDs are generated without the consent each one depends on
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Alternative identifiers are built from authenticated personal data, so generating one for a user who has not given the consent it requires creates a compliance exposure under frameworks such as the IAB Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF), not only a data-quality issue. An ID generated outside its consent basis can be dropped downstream or carry regulatory risk, so the signal is both legally fraught and unreliable.

educate.base:
Every alternative identifier in this subcategory is built from personal data. RampID resolves an authenticated, consented signal into a pseudonymous identifier, and Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) and EUID are derived from a hashed email or phone number. Because the input is personal data, the legal basis for generating and passing the identifier matters as much as the technical setup. In the European Economic Area (EEA) and the United Kingdom, that basis is usually established through the IAB Transparency and Consent Framework, which records the user's choices in a consent string that downstream vendors read.

The failure this check looks for is an identifier generated without the consent it depends on. The identity module should call out to its vendor only after the relevant consent purposes are granted, e.g., the purpose covering storing and accessing information on the device. If the module fires before the consent management platform has resolved, or ignores the consent string entirely, the identifier gets built for users who never agreed to it. Prebid's User ID module enforces this by checking the consent string and skipping the external vendor call when the required purpose is not granted, so a setup that bypasses that gate is working against the framework.

The consequence runs in two directions. Downstream, a buyer or supply-side platform that honors the consent string may drop an identifier that arrives without a matching basis, so the signal that took effort to generate gets discarded anyway. Upstream, generating identifiers outside their consent basis is the regulatory exposure itself, independent of whether any buyer uses the result. Aligning each identifier to its consent gate protects both the signal and the publisher.

A few signs point to identifiers generated without aligned consent:
  • The identity module fires before the consent management platform resolves, so the ID call runs ahead of the user's choices
  • An identifier appears in bid requests for users whose consent string does not grant the purpose the ID depends on
  • The setup has no consent gate on the ID call, so the identifier is generated regardless of the signal in the consent string
You may want to confirm that each alternative ID is generated only after the consent it depends on is granted. One approach is to test the site under a consent string that withholds the relevant purpose and confirm the identifier is absent from the bid request, then test under full consent and confirm it appears.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site serving EEA and UK readers, the TCF consent string governs whether an authenticated identifier may be built, and a registration wall that captures an email does not by itself establish that basis. You may want to confirm the identity module reads the consent string and holds the ID call until the relevant purpose is granted, separately from the login step.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the email captured at checkout is often used to build an alternative identifier, so the consent collected at that step has to cover advertising use, not only order fulfillment. You may want to confirm the consent basis for the identifier is distinct from the transactional consent and that the ID call respects it.

learn_more.source_title: User ID, GDPR, Permissions, and Opt-Out

investigate.intro: Test whether each alternative identifier respects the consent it depends on by comparing what appears in a bid request under a withholding consent string against a full-consent one.

browser_devtools.label: Browser developer tools
steps: Load the site and decline or withhold the relevant consent purpose in the consent management platform, then inspect a bid request in the 'Network' tab.
Confirm the alternative identifiers are absent from the EIDs array when the consent string does not grant the purpose they depend on.
Reload with full consent granted and confirm the identifiers now appear, which shows the consent gate is governing the ID call.
Record any identifier that appears under the withholding consent string, which is generated outside its consent basis.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Review how the identity setup is tied to the consent management platform and record whether the ID calls are gated on the consent signal.
Confirm the order of operations places the consent resolution before the identity module fires.
Record any identifier whose generation is not conditioned on the consent purpose it depends on.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each alternative ID, whether it was absent under a consent string that withholds the relevant purpose and present under full consent. Identifiers that appear only when their consent purpose is granted pass. An identifier that appears under a withholding consent string, or whose ID call fires before the consent management platform resolves, is a fail recorded with the identifier and the consent gap found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether each identifier was absent under a withholding consent string and present under full consent above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} generates alternative identifiers without aligning them to the consent each one depends on, so identifiers built from personal data can be created for users who did not grant the relevant purpose. Under frameworks such as the IAB Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF), that is a compliance exposure, and downstream partners that honor the consent string may drop the identifier anyway, so the signal is both legally fraught and unreliable. {{voice_recommend}} gating each identity call on the consent string so the identifier is generated only after its purpose is granted, then testing the site under a withholding consent string to confirm the identifier is absent and under full consent to confirm it appears. After the change, recheck both states to confirm each identifier respects the consent it depends on.
